original_abstract:
In the management of high-dimensional credit card transaction data, feature selection is a critical preprocessing step used to optimize the performance of fraud detection models. This study provides a comparative analysis of two prominent feature selection strategies: SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values and models’ built-in feature importance mechanisms. Both techniques rank features to identify the most predictive subsets for model assessment. To evaluate these methods, we developed classification models using five distinct learners—XGBoost, Decision Tree, CatBoost, Extremely Randomized Trees, and Random Forest—measuring their effectiveness via the Area under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC). 

Experimental results and statistical analyses conducted on the Kaggle Credit Card Fraud Detection Dataset reveal that built-in importance-based selection generally outperforms SHAP-based methods across various classifiers and feature subset sizes. Given these findings, we recommend the use of a model's inherent feature importance for large-scale datasets. This approach is not only more effective but also more computationally efficient; while SHAP requires a distinct and often intensive post-hoc calculation, built-in importance is generated naturally during the training process at no additional cost. Consequently, utilizing integrated importance metrics offers a more practical and robust framework for detecting fraud in complex, high-volume financial environments.
humanized_abstract:
In high-dimensional credit card transaction data, feature selection is always a crucial step before building any models, which decides the future performance of the model. So, here we are comparing the performances of two widely used methods of feature selection: SHAP values and feature importance. SHAP and feature importance help us to rank the features and decide which are the important features in the data which are to be considered by the model. Here, we are building a classification model on the data using five different machine learning classifiers (XGBoost, Decision Tree, CatBoost, Extremely Randomized Trees and Random Forest) by considering all the features and all the selected features. We calculated the performance of the models on test dataset by evaluating the Area under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC) metric.

From my experiments and some statistical analysis performed on Kaggle Credit Card Fraud Detection Dataset, I found out that generally the built-in importance-based selection is better than SHAP values for many classifiers and feature subset sizes. For large-scale datasets, such as the credit card transactions, I recommend to use the built-in model feature importance for faster and more accurate results. The SHAP values will require a post-hoc analysis that can be very time consuming. The built-in model feature importance on the other hand was already generated during the building of the model and does not require any extra computation costs. So, overall, I think that using the integrated importance metrics will be the best choice for a real-world application for detecting the credit card transactions fraud in such large-scale financial systems.

document.input:
In the management of high-dimensional credit card transaction data, feature selection is a critical preprocessing step used to optimize the performance of fraud detection models.
